name: Product Analytics
blurb: Metrics, funnels, retention, A/B — analyst → product.

lessons.title: The vocabulary of a product analyst: active users, activation, retention, churn
lessons.concept:
Product analytics starts with a humbling idea: a download is not usage, and a sign-up is not value. If marketing celebrates '1 lakh installs' but only 4,000 people opened the app this week, you don't have a hit — you have a leaky bucket. So the first job of a product analyst is to count *active* users: DAU (daily active), WAU (weekly active) and MAU (monthly active). The catch is that 'active' is a definition you must write down. For a PhonePe-style wallet, 'active' might mean 'completed at least one transaction'; for a content app it might mean 'watched one video'. Two analysts using different definitions will report different DAU for the very same app.

Once you can count active users, four words describe almost everything leadership cares about. *Stickiness* (DAU ÷ MAU) tells you how many days a month your typical user shows up. *Activation* is the moment a new user first experiences real value — the 'aha' — such as a first successful order on a quick-commerce app. *Retention* is the share of a group that keeps coming back after N days, and its mirror image, *churn*, is the share that stops. A company can pour crores into acquisition, but if retention is weak, growth quietly stalls because you refill the bucket only as fast as it leaks.

KPIs (key performance indicators) are the handful of these numbers you actually steer by. The beginner's mistake is tracking 40 metrics and steering by none. Good KPIs are few, clearly defined, and tied to real value delivered — so that when the number moves, everyone knows what it means and what to do about it.
lessons.example: You join a Swiggy-style food app. Marketing reports 5,00,000 MAU. You dig in: DAU is 50,000, so stickiness = 50,000 ÷ 5,00,000 = 10% — the average user orders on roughly 3 days a month. Of 20,000 new sign-ups last month, 8,000 placed a first order within 7 days, so activation = 40%. The other 60% installed, browsed and vanished — a far more useful headline for the product team than the 5-lakh MAU banner.
lessons.code:
Stickiness (DAU/MAU) = DAU / MAU
  e.g. 50,000 / 5,00,000 = 0.10 = 10%
  Rough reading: 0.10 x 30 ~ 3 active days per user per month

Activation rate = users who hit the 'aha' event / users who signed up
  e.g. 8,000 / 20,000 = 40%

Monthly churn = users lost in month / users active at start of month
  e.g. 1,200 lost / 10,000 = 12% churn
Monthly retention = 1 - churn = 88%

Excel:
  Stickiness  =B2/B3
  Activation  =COUNTIFS(status,"activated")/COUNTA(signup_ids)
  Churn       =(start_users - retained_users)/start_users
lessons.tryit: Open an app you use daily (Zomato, Cred, PhonePe). Write a one-line definition of what 'active' should mean for it. Then invent plausible numbers — MAU 2,00,000 and DAU 30,000 — and calculate stickiness, translating it into 'active days per month'. Finally, name one activation event and one KPI you'd put on the team's dashboard.

mistakes.m: Reporting installs or sign-ups as if they were usage.
mistakes.fix: Always separate acquisition (installs) from engagement (active users). Lead with DAU/MAU and activation, not download counts.

mistakes.m: Leaving 'active' undefined, so DAU means different things to different teams.
mistakes.fix: Write a one-line definition of the active event and store it with the metric, e.g. 'active = opened app AND viewed >=1 screen'.

mistakes.m: Confusing stickiness with retention.
mistakes.fix: Stickiness (DAU/MAU) measures intensity within a period; retention measures whether a specific cohort returns over time. Report both — they answer different questions.

quiz.q: An app has MAU 4,00,000 and DAU 40,000. What is its stickiness and roughly how many days a month is the average user active?
opts: 10% — the average user is active about 3 days a month
10% — the average user is active about 10 days a month
40% — the average user is active about 12 days a month
1% — the average user is active less than a day a month
quiz.why: Stickiness = DAU/MAU = 40,000/4,00,000 = 0.10 = 10%. Multiplying by ~30 days gives 0.10 x 30 = 3 active days per user per month.
quiz.approach: Divide DAU by MAU to get stickiness, then multiply the ratio by ~30 for a rough estimate of active days per month.

quiz.q: For a quick-commerce grocery app, which is the best event to track as 'activation'?
opts: Number of app installs from the Play Store
A new user completing their first successful order
Total monthly revenue
Average session length across all users
quiz.why: Activation is a new user's first experience of core value — here, a first successful order. Installs are acquisition; revenue and session length are different metrics.
quiz.approach: Activation is the earliest 'aha' action that reliably signals a new user got real value and is likely to stay — pick that specific first success.

quiz.q: If monthly churn is 8%, what is retention and what does churn measure?
opts: Retention 92%; churn is the share of users who stopped using the product in the period
Retention 8%; churn is the number of new sign-ups
Retention 108%; churn is total revenue lost
Retention 80%; churn is the same as stickiness
quiz.why: Retention = 1 - churn = 1 - 0.08 = 92%. Churn is the proportion of users active at the start of the period who stopped by the end.
quiz.approach: Retention and churn are complements over the same period and sum to 100% — subtract churn from 100% to get retention.

lessons.title: The AARRR pirate funnel and choosing a North Star Metric
lessons.concept:
Around 2007 the investor Dave McClure compressed a whole startup's health into five letters that happen to sound like a pirate's growl: AARRR. They stand for Acquisition (how do people find you?), Activation (do they have a great first experience?), Retention (do they come back?), Referral (do they tell others?) and Revenue (do they pay?). Read top to bottom it's a funnel: strangers become users, users become fans, fans become paying customers. For a Meesho- or Urban-Company-style app, Acquisition might be a WhatsApp share or a Google ad, Activation the first order, Retention a repeat order in 30 days, Referral inviting a friend, and Revenue the take-rate on each transaction.

The funnel only works if the events are actually logged. This is *instrumentation*: before a feature ships, analysts and engineers agree a tracking plan — a list of events (e.g. `signup_completed`, `first_order_placed`, `order_repeated`) with consistent names and properties. If nobody logged `first_order_placed`, no dashboard on earth can tell you your activation rate later. 'Garbage in, garbage out' is the analyst's daily reality, so getting the event taxonomy right is as important as the analysis itself.

Staring at five stages, leadership still asks: 'what is the *one* number we're all rowing towards?' That is the North Star Metric — the single measure that best captures the core value your product delivers, chosen so that when it rises, customers are genuinely better off. Airbnb is often described as watching 'nights booked'; a messaging app might pick 'messages sent'. A good North Star is a *value* metric, not a vanity metric like installs, and usually not raw revenue — because revenue can climb in the short term even while you erode the customer experience.
lessons.example: Mapping a beauty-services app (think Urban Company) onto AARRR: Acquisition = installs from a festival-season ad; Activation = first service booked and completed; Retention = a second booking within 60 days; Referral = 'refer a friend, get Rs 200 off'; Revenue = commission on each booking. The team debates a North Star and lands on 'completed bookings per week' — it only rises when real customers get real haircuts and cleanings, so it can't be inflated by ad spend the way installs can.
lessons.code:
AARRR funnel — worked example (one month)
Stage        Metric                    Users      Stage conversion
Acquisition  installs                  1,00,000     —
Activation   first order placed        25,000     25% of installs
Retention    repeat order in 30 days   10,000     40% of activated
Referral     sent >=1 referral         2,000      20% of retained
Revenue      paying (net of refunds)   9,500      95% of retained

Stage conversion = users at stage / users at previous stage

North Star candidates (choose a VALUE metric):
  good:  weekly completed orders, weekly active buyers
  weak:  total installs (vanity), one-off campaign revenue
lessons.tryit: Pick an Indian app you know well and map all five AARRR stages to a concrete event for it (Acquisition = ..., through Revenue = ...). Then propose one North Star Metric and write one sentence defending why it captures real customer value rather than vanity. Finally, list the 3 events you'd ask engineering to log so you could actually measure your funnel.

mistakes.m: Pouring budget into Acquisition while Retention leaks — the classic 'leaky bucket'.
mistakes.fix: Fix activation and retention before scaling spend. If the repeat-order rate flatlines, new users won't compound into growth.

mistakes.m: Picking revenue or installs as the North Star Metric.
mistakes.fix: Choose a metric that captures delivered value (e.g. completed orders, watch-time for a content app). Revenue and installs can rise even as customer value falls.

mistakes.m: Trying to analyse a funnel you never instrumented.
mistakes.fix: Agree a tracking plan with engineering before launch, with consistent event names like `first_order_placed`, so the data exists when you need it.

links.t: Dave McClure (creator of pirate metrics) — Wikipedia

quiz.q: In Dave McClure's pirate metrics, what do the five letters of AARRR stand for, in order?
opts: Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue
Awareness, Acquisition, Retention, Revenue, Referral
Acquisition, Adoption, Revenue, Retention, Referral
Activation, Acquisition, Referral, Retention, Revenue
quiz.why: AARRR = Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — a funnel running from first touch to money, with Referral just before Revenue.
quiz.approach: Walk the funnel from how people find you (Acquisition) down to how they pay (Revenue), remembering the two R's are Retention then Referral.

quiz.q: Which is the best North Star Metric for a video-streaming app?
opts: Number of app installs this quarter
Total weekly time spent watching content
Marketing spend per month
Total number of videos in the catalogue
quiz.why: A North Star should capture the value users receive. Weekly watch-time rises only when people genuinely enjoy content. Installs and catalogue size are vanity/supply metrics, and marketing spend is an input — none of them measures value actually delivered to viewers.
quiz.approach: Ask which single number, when it goes up, means customers truly got more value — not more supply or more spend — and that is your North Star.

quiz.q: You want to measure activation rate next quarter, but the `first_order_placed` event was never logged. What is the core lesson?
opts: You can reconstruct activation perfectly from install counts
Instrumentation must be planned before launch; un-logged events can't be analysed later
Activation doesn't need event data
The revenue dashboard already contains activation
quiz.why: Analysis can only use data that was captured. If the event was never instrumented, that history simply doesn't exist and no dashboard can recover it.
quiz.approach: Remember that data must be logged at the time it happens — decide the tracking plan up front, before the feature ships.

lessons.title: Cohorts, retention curves and reading a product dashboard
lessons.concept:
One retention percentage for the whole app is like one average temperature for all of India — technically true, useless for packing. Cohort analysis fixes this by grouping users by when they first arrived — the 'January sign-ups', the 'week-32 sign-ups' — and then following each group forward in time. Because everyone in a cohort started together, you can fairly ask 'did the people who joined *after* we redesigned onboarding come back more than those who joined before?' Blended numbers can't answer that; cohorts can.

The classic view is the retention curve: weeks (or days) since sign-up on the x-axis, and the percentage of that cohort still active on the y-axis. Real curves drop steeply at first — most casual triers leave — and the question is what happens next. A curve that keeps sliding towards zero means nobody forms a habit. A curve that *flattens* at, say, 20% means one in five users found durable value; that flattening is one of the most watched signals of product-market fit. Teams track checkpoints like Day-1, Day-7 and Day-30 retention, and take care to distinguish classic Day-N retention (active exactly on day N) from rolling retention (active on day N or any day after).

Reading a product dashboard is then a discipline of asking three questions before you trust a number. What is the denominator? What is the exact date range? And are these new users, returning users, or both mixed together? A headline that 'retention fell to 55%' may simply mean a marketing blast brought in a wave of low-intent new users this week — each existing cohort is fine, but the blend looks worse. Segment before you panic.
lessons.example: A UPI-bills app builds a weekly cohort table. The cohort that signed up the week onboarding was simplified shows Week-1 retention of 48% versus 33% for the previous cohort — a real improvement you'd never see in the blended '38% overall' figure. Meanwhile the Day-30 column for last week's cohort is blank, because that cohort is only 8 days old: you simply cannot report a 30-day number for users who haven't lived 30 days yet.
lessons.code:
Weekly retention cohort table (% of cohort still active)
Cohort (sign-up week) | Size  | Wk0  | Wk1 | Wk2 | Wk3
2026-06-17            | 5,000 | 100% | 42% | 31% | 27%
2026-06-24            | 5,400 | 100% | 45% | 33% | 28%
2026-07-01            | 6,100 | 100% | 48% | 35% |  -   (too new for Wk3)

Wk-N retention = active users from cohort in week N / original cohort size
(As of 2026-07-18 the 2026-07-01 cohort is only ~17 days old, so its Wk3
 checkpoint at day 21 hasn't happened yet — leave that cell blank.)

Excel (one cell of the triangle):
  =COUNTIFS(signup_week,$A2, active_week,"Wk"&B$1) / cohort_size

A curve that flattens (weekly drops shrinking toward a floor near ~27%,
instead of sliding to 0%) hints at product-market fit.
lessons.tryit: Build a tiny 3-cohort weekly retention table in Excel or Google Sheets with invented but realistic numbers. Fill Wk0/Wk1/Wk2, leave any 'too new' cells blank, and write one sentence on whether your newest cohort's curve looks like it's flattening. Note which single cell you would NOT report yet, and why.

mistakes.m: Reporting one blended retention number and calling it the trend.
mistakes.fix: Break users into join-date cohorts. A change in new-user volume can move the blend even when every individual cohort is stable or improving.

mistakes.m: Reading a Day-30 figure for a cohort younger than 30 days.
mistakes.fix: Only report a checkpoint once the whole cohort has aged into it; leave younger cells blank rather than showing a misleading partial number.

mistakes.m: Trusting a dashboard number without checking its denominator and date range.
mistakes.fix: Before quoting any metric, confirm what it's divided by, the exact date window, and whether it counts new, returning, or all users.

links.t: Create a PivotTable to analyze data (Excel) — Microsoft Support

quiz.q: A retention curve that flattens above zero (instead of sliding to zero) most likely indicates what?
opts: A bug in the tracking code
A core of users who found lasting value — a signal of product-market fit
That the product has no active users
That churn is exactly 100%
quiz.why: Flattening means a stable base of users keeps returning period after period rather than all churning — one of the classic signals of product-market fit.
quiz.approach: Look at where the curve settles: settling above zero means a durable habit formed; sliding to zero means none did.

quiz.q: Blended retention dropped this week, yet every individual cohort improved. What is the most likely explanation?
opts: Every existing user churned
A wave of new, lower-intent users entered and diluted the blended average
Product-market fit has just been confirmed
The denominator is irrelevant
quiz.why: New users naturally have lower early retention. A surge of them drags the blended average down even while each cohort, measured on its own, is improving — a mix shift.
quiz.approach: When blended and per-cohort trends disagree, suspect a change in the mix of users, then segment by cohort to see the real story.

quiz.q: A cohort signed up just 9 days ago. Which figure is legitimate to report for it today?
opts: Its Day-30 retention
Its Day-1 and Day-7 retention
Its Day-90 retention
Its lifetime revenue over the next year
quiz.why: Only checkpoints the cohort has already aged into can be measured. At 9 days old it has passed Day-1 and Day-7 but not Day-30 or Day-90.
quiz.approach: Report a checkpoint only once enough calendar time has passed for the entire cohort to reach it.

lessons.title: Experiment and prioritise: A/B testing, RICE/ICE, and telling the data story
lessons.concept:
Sooner or later you stop guessing and run an experiment. An A/B test randomly splits users into a control group (today's experience) and a treatment group (the new idea), so the only systematic difference between them is your change. You choose one *primary* metric you're trying to move — say checkout conversion — and a set of *guardrail* metrics you must not harm along the way, like crash rate, page latency, refund rate or unsubscribes. A change that lifts add-to-cart clicks but quietly raises crashes is a loss, not a win.

Three numbers keep tests honest. You fix the *sample size* before you start, based on the baseline rate, the minimum effect worth detecting (MDE), a significance level (usually 5%) and power (usually 80%) — bigger effects need fewer users, tiny effects need many. You judge results by statistical significance rather than eyeballing a chart. And you resist *peeking*: repeatedly checking a running test and stopping the instant it looks significant dramatically inflates false positives, because with enough looks, random noise will eventually cross the line. Decide the sample size and duration up front, then wait.

Off the test bench, a product analyst also helps decide *what to build next*, and two lightweight frameworks make that transparent. RICE scores each idea as (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort; ICE is the quicker cousin, Impact x Confidence x Ease. Neither is precise science — they are structured arguments that stop the loudest voice in the room from always winning. Finally, none of this matters if leadership doesn't act, so you tell a data *story*: lead with the headline (bottom line up front), then the 'so what', then a clear recommendation — and pitch the depth to the audience, because a PM wants the mechanism while a VP wants the decision.
lessons.example: You test a one-tap checkout button. Baseline conversion is 10%, and you want to detect at least a 2-point lift. The rough sample-size rule says about 3,600 users per group. After the pre-agreed two weeks, treatment converts at 12.1% (significant) while crash rate and refund rate — your guardrails — are unchanged. Your slide to leadership doesn't open with methodology; it opens with: 'One-tap checkout lifted conversion from 10.0% to 12.1% with no hit to crashes or refunds — recommend rolling out to 100%.'
lessons.code:
A/B sample size — rule of thumb (per variant, ~5% significance, 80% power):
  n ~ 16 x p x (1 - p) / (MDE)^2
  p = baseline rate, MDE = absolute lift you want to detect
  e.g. p = 0.10, MDE = 0.02:
       n ~ 16 x 0.10 x 0.90 / 0.02^2 = 1.44 / 0.0004 = 3,600 per group

Peeking: checking daily and stopping at the first 'p < 0.05' pushes the true
false-positive rate well above 5%. Fix sample size and duration in advance.

RICE = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort
  Reach = users affected per period; Impact = 3/2/1/0.5/0.25;
  Confidence = 100%/80%/50%; Effort = person-months
  Idea A: (8,000 x 2 x 0.8) / 4 = 3,200
  Idea B: (2,000 x 3 x 1.0) / 1 = 6,000   -> build B first

ICE = Impact x Confidence x Ease   (each rated 1-10; quicker, rougher than RICE)
lessons.tryit: Take a real feature idea for an app you use. (1) Score it with RICE — estimate Reach, Impact, Confidence and Effort, then compute the score. (2) Design an A/B test for it: name the primary metric, two guardrail metrics, and use n ~ 16 x p x (1-p) / MDE^2 to estimate the sample size per group for a baseline you choose. (3) Write the single opening sentence you'd say to leadership if the test wins.

mistakes.m: Peeking at a live test and stopping the moment p < 0.05.
mistakes.fix: Fix the sample size and run-time before launch and decide only at the end, or use a proper sequential-testing method. Early stopping on significance inflates false positives.

mistakes.m: Optimising the primary metric while ignoring guardrail metrics.
mistakes.fix: Always define guardrails (crashes, latency, revenue, unsubscribes) and block any launch that harms them, even when the primary metric wins.

mistakes.m: Presenting a wall of charts with no recommendation.
mistakes.fix: Lead with the bottom line and a specific recommendation; move methodology and raw data to an appendix. Leaders act on decisions, not data dumps.

quiz.q: Why is 'peeking' — repeatedly checking a running test and stopping as soon as it looks significant — a problem?
opts: It saves money and is considered best practice
It inflates the false-positive rate, because random noise eventually crosses the significance line
It has no effect on the results
It makes the required sample size larger than needed
quiz.why: Each extra look is another chance for random fluctuation to cross p < 0.05. Many looks mean the true Type I error rate rises well above the nominal 5%.
quiz.approach: Recognise that more looks equal more chances for noise to fake a win; fix the sample size and duration in advance and decide only once.

quiz.q: Idea A scores RICE = (8,000 x 2 x 0.8)/4 and Idea B scores RICE = (2,000 x 3 x 1.0)/1. Which do you build first and why?
opts: A, because it reaches more users
B, because its RICE score (6,000) beats A's (3,200)
A, because higher reach always wins
Neither; RICE cannot compare ideas
quiz.why: A = 3,200 and B = 6,000. RICE ranks by the final score, not by any single input; B's low effort plus high impact and confidence outweigh A's larger reach.
quiz.approach: Compute (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort for each idea and rank by the resulting score, not by reach alone.

quiz.q: What is the best opening line for a data story delivered to a busy VP?
opts: Here are 20 charts of everything we logged this quarter
One-tap checkout lifted conversion 10.0% -> 12.1% with no guardrail harm — recommend full rollout
Let me first walk through the SQL and the event schema
We are not sure yet what the data shows
quiz.why: Bottom line up front: lead with the headline result and a clear recommendation. Methodology and detail belong later, for those who ask.
quiz.approach: Put the decision and the single most important number first; save the SQL, schema and caveats for the appendix.

lessons.title: Product Metrics & Choosing a North Star
lessons.concept:
When you move from a general BI analyst into a product role at an Indian startup or marketplace, your vocabulary changes. Instead of "revenue by region" you start living inside a handful of behavioural metrics. The four you meet first are Active Users (DAU/MAU), Activation, Retention and Churn. A crucial subtlety: an "active" user is not someone who merely opened the app — it is someone who performed a meaningful action you defined (placed an order, made a UPI payment, watched a lesson). Stickiness = DAU / MAU tells you what fraction of your monthly users show up on a given day. A hyper-daily product like Swiggy or Zepto should have high stickiness; a monthly-use product like an income-tax filing app or ClearTax will naturally be low, and that is fine — the benchmark depends on the job the product does.

Activation is the moment a new user first experiences real value — the "aha moment" (first successful order delivered, first ₹ sent on PhonePe, first stock bought on Groww). Retention is whether they keep coming back; Churn is the mirror image — the share of users who stop. A simple period churn rate = users lost during the period ÷ users at the start of the period. Acquisition without activation and retention is a leaky bucket: you pour crores of marketing spend into installs that never convert to habit.

A North Star Metric (a framing popularised by Sean Ellis, the same growth thinker who coined "growth hacking") is the single number that best captures the core value your product delivers to customers — and, ideally, leads rather than lags revenue. The test of a good North Star: if it goes up, customers are genuinely getting more value, and the business benefits over time. Downloads, registrations and funding raised are vanity or lagging numbers; "monthly orders delivered" or "weekly active learners completing a lesson" are value metrics. A strong North Star is paired with a small set of supporting input metrics and at least one guardrail (a metric that must not degrade, like delivery time or refund rate).
lessons.example: A Bengaluru edtech startup proudly puts "5 million downloads" on its investor deck. But when you build the real dashboard, only 40,000 users complete a lesson in a typical week, and of a January signup cohort just 12% are still learning by March. Downloads is a vanity metric; the honest North Star here is "weekly active learners who complete at least one lesson", with course-completion rate as a guardrail so the team doesn't chase logins by making lessons trivially short.
lessons.code:
Stickiness (Excel):
  Stickiness % = DAU / MAU
  =120000/400000  ->  0.30  =>  30% of monthly users active on an average day

Period churn rate:
  Churn % = Users_lost_in_period / Users_at_start_of_period
  =(50000-46000)/50000  ->  0.08  =>  8% monthly churn

Retention (complement of churn for that period):
  Retention % = 1 - Churn %  =  1 - 0.08  =  92%

Rule of thumb: pick ONE North Star + 3 input metrics + 1 guardrail.
  North Star : monthly orders delivered
  Inputs     : new-buyer activation %, repeat rate, catalogue size
  Guardrail  : average delivery time / refund rate
lessons.tryit: Pick one Indian app you use daily (Swiggy, PhonePe, Groww, Meesho...). Write down: (1) the single North Star Metric you think its product team tracks, (2) three input metrics that feed into it, and (3) one guardrail metric that must not get worse. In two sentences, justify why your North Star reflects real customer value rather than a vanity number.

mistakes.m: Counting anyone who opens the app as an 'active user', which inflates DAU/MAU and hides a retention problem.
mistakes.fix: Define 'active' as a meaningful value action (order placed, payment made, lesson completed) and instrument that specific event.

mistakes.m: Confusing Acquisition with Activation — celebrating installs while most new users never reach the aha moment.
mistakes.fix: Track an explicit activation event and measure the % of new users who reach it within their first session or first N days.

mistakes.m: Choosing revenue, downloads or funding as the North Star Metric.
mistakes.fix: Pick a leading value metric that customers directly experience; let revenue be the downstream outcome it drives, not the North Star itself.

quiz.q: An app reports DAU of 90,000 and MAU of 300,000. What is its stickiness?
quiz.why: Stickiness = DAU / MAU = 90,000 / 300,000 = 0.30 = 30% — about 30% of monthly users show up on an average day. Inverting to MAU/DAU gives 3.33 (misread as 3.3% or 300%); dividing DAU by the non-daily remainder, 90,000 / 210,000, gives the tempting-but-wrong 43%.
quiz.approach: Divide daily active users by monthly active users and read it as a percentage; it is always between 0% and 100%, so anything above 100% can be ruled out immediately.

quiz.q: Which is the best North Star Metric for a food-delivery marketplace?
opts: Total lifetime app downloads
Monthly orders successfully delivered
Number of push notifications sent
Total funding raised this year
quiz.why: Orders delivered captures recurring customer value and leads revenue; downloads and funding are vanity/lagging, and notifications sent is an activity output, not customer value.
quiz.approach: Ask: if this number rises, are customers genuinely getting more value AND does the business benefit over time? Only a value metric passes both tests.

quiz.q: A user installs the app, opens it, browses, but never completes a first order. Which stage did they fail?
opts: Acquisition
Activation
Referral
Revenue
quiz.why: They were successfully acquired (installed and opened) but never reached the aha moment of a first order, so they failed Activation.
quiz.approach: Map the user's furthest step onto the funnel: reaching the app = acquired; reaching first value = activated. The gap between the two names the failure.

lessons.title: The AARRR Funnel, Event Tracking & Cohort Retention
lessons.concept:
The AARRR framework — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — is a mental model for the whole customer lifecycle, coined by Dave McClure (who first presented it in 2007 in a talk titled "Startup Metrics for Pirates") and nicknamed the "pirate metrics" (say the letters out loud). For a marketplace like Meesho, Acquisition is how a reseller first arrives (ads, WhatsApp shares), Activation is her first successful order, Retention is whether she keeps ordering week after week, Referral is her inviting other resellers, and Revenue is the margin the platform earns. Reading your funnel stage by stage tells you where the biggest leak is — and fixing the biggest leak, not the most fun one, is where product analytics earns its keep.

None of this analysis is possible unless the product is instrumented — every meaningful action logged as an event with properties. An event is a verb ("order_placed"); properties are the adjectives (amount, category, payment_method, city). The discipline that separates good analysts from frustrated ones is a consistent naming taxonomy agreed before you build the dashboard: object_action in snake_case, a fixed set of properties, and no duplicate events like "OrderPlaced", "order-placed" and "placed_order" all meaning the same thing. Tools you will meet in India include GA4, Mixpanel, Amplitude and CleverTap. Track meaningful actions, not everything — event bloat makes analysis slower and dashboards untrustworthy.

Retention is best read not as one number but as a curve, and the tool for that is cohort analysis. You group users by the week (or day) they first signed up, then track what fraction return in week 1, week 2, week 3 and so on. Plotted, healthy products show a curve that declines and then flattens onto a positive plateau — the "smiling" or flattening retention curve — which signals a stable core of users who found lasting value (a common qualitative signal of product-market fit). A curve that decays to near zero means you have a leaky bucket no amount of acquisition spend can fill. Be clear about your definition: N-day retention (active exactly on day N) is stricter than unbounded/rolling retention (active on day N or later).
lessons.example: Zepto's growth team builds a weekly cohort table. The cohort that signed up in the first week of June shows: Week 0 = 100%, Week 1 = 46%, Week 2 = 33%, Week 3 = 30%, Week 4 = 29%. The curve is clearly flattening around 29-30% — a healthy core of repeat grocery buyers. A rival's cohort instead reads 100% → 25% → 9% → 3% → 1%, decaying to nothing: their marketing spend is filling a leaky bucket, and the fix is retention, not more installs.
lessons.code:
Cohort retention (each cell = returning users / cohort size):
  Signups (Week-0 cohort) = 1000
  Active in Week 1 = 400  ->  D/W1 retention = 400/1000 = 40%
  Active in Week 2 = 250  ->  W2 retention   = 250/1000 = 25%
  Active in Week 4 = 180  ->  W4 retention   = 180/1000 = 18%

Excel/Sheets to count a cohort that returned in a given week:
  cohort size:  =COUNTIFS(signup_week, "2026-W23")
  returned W2 :  =COUNTIFS(signup_week, "2026-W23", active_week, "2026-W25")
  retention   :  =returned_W2 / cohort_size

Note: denominator is ALWAYS the original cohort size, never the previous week.
lessons.tryit: Take any list of user signups with a signup date and later activity dates (make up 20 rows if needed). Bucket users into 4 weekly cohorts and, using COUNTIFS, build a 4-column retention table (W1–W4) where every cell is divided by that cohort's original size. Then say in one line whether the curve is flattening (healthy) or decaying to zero (leaky).

mistakes.m: Obsessing over the top of the funnel (Acquisition) while retention leaks — pouring spend into a leaky bucket.
mistakes.fix: Find the biggest drop-off stage in AARRR first; a 5-point retention gain usually beats a 5-point acquisition gain economically.

mistakes.m: Inconsistent event names ('OrderPlaced' vs 'order_placed') and untracked properties, making the funnel impossible to trust.
mistakes.fix: Agree a naming taxonomy (object_action, snake_case) and a fixed property schema before instrumenting, and document it.

mistakes.m: Reading retention as a single headline number instead of a curve, hiding whether it stabilises or collapses.
mistakes.fix: Plot the full cohort retention curve over several weeks and check whether it flattens onto a positive plateau.

links.t: Dave McClure (coined AARRR / pirate metrics) — Wikipedia

quiz.q: What do the letters in the AARRR pirate funnel stand for, in order?
opts: Awareness, Acquisition, Retention, Revenue, Referral
Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue
Activation, Acquisition, Revenue, Retention, Referral
Acquisition, Adoption, Retention, Revenue, Referral
quiz.why: AARRR = Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — the five customer-lifecycle stages Dave McClure introduced in 2007 in his "Startup Metrics for Pirates" talk.
quiz.approach: Walk a single user through their journey: they arrive (Acquisition), hit first value (Activation), come back (Retention), invite others (Referral), and pay (Revenue).

quiz.q: A cohort's retention curve declines then flattens onto a positive plateau (e.g. levels off near 30%). What does this most likely indicate?
opts: No product-market fit; the product is failing
A stable core of retained users — a common signal of product-market fit
A data error, because retention should always rise
That all users have churned
quiz.why: A flattening curve on a positive value means a durable core keeps coming back after early drop-off, which is a qualitative signal of product-market fit.
quiz.approach: Ignore the early decline — everyone loses casual triers. Watch the tail: a positive plateau is good, a decay to zero is a leaky bucket.

quiz.q: Which is the best practice for event instrumentation?
opts: Log every possible click with whatever name is handy at the time
Track meaningful actions using a consistent naming taxonomy and defined properties
Track only page views, since actions are too complex
Never attach properties to events to keep data small
quiz.why: Meaningful, consistently named events with a fixed property schema make funnels and cohorts trustworthy; ad-hoc naming and event bloat make analysis unreliable.
quiz.approach: Instrument for the questions you'll ask: define a taxonomy first, log value actions with rich but standardised properties, and avoid duplicate/junk events.

lessons.title: A/B Testing Products: Sample Size, Guardrails & Peeking
lessons.concept:
An A/B test (a controlled online experiment) randomly splits users into a control group (current experience) and one or more variants (the change), then compares a pre-declared primary metric. Randomisation is what lets you attribute a difference to the change rather than to who happened to see it. Alongside the primary metric you must declare guardrail metrics — numbers the change must NOT harm even if the primary metric improves. If a new one-tap checkout lifts conversion but doubles the crash rate, inflates page-load latency, or spikes refunds, that is not a win. Guardrails protect you from local optimisations that damage the whole product.

Before launching, you compute a sample size from three ingredients: the baseline rate, the Minimum Detectable Effect (MDE — the smallest lift worth caring about), and your desired statistical power (usually 80%) and significance level (usually 5%). A practical rule of thumb for a proportion metric is n ≈ 16 · p · (1 − p) / δ² per variant, where p is the baseline conversion and δ is the absolute MDE. The constant 16 comes from the usual 80% power and 5% two-sided significance ((1.96 + 0.84)² × 2 ≈ 16). Skipping this step is the classic error: an underpowered test that shows "no significant difference" often means the test never had a chance of detecting the effect, not that the effect is absent. Smaller effects need dramatically more users — halving the MDE roughly quadruples the required sample.

The most seductive trap is peeking: watching a live test and stopping the moment p-value dips below 0.05. Because the p-value fluctuates as data arrives, repeatedly testing and stopping on a favourable dip massively inflates the false-positive (Type I error) rate — you "find" wins that are noise. The disciplined approach is to fix the sample size and duration in advance and only evaluate at the end, or to use a method explicitly designed for continuous monitoring (sequential testing). Equally, never run or read a test across an anomaly like a Diwali or Big Billion Days sale: seasonality contaminates both groups' behaviour and your baseline. Run for full weekly cycles so weekday/weekend mix is balanced.
lessons.example: A CRED PM wants to test a new rewards button. Baseline claim-conversion is 10%, and the team decides a 2 percentage-point lift (to 12%) is the smallest change worth shipping. Plugging in, they need roughly 3,600 users per variant — about 7,200 total — and they commit to running two full weeks. Three days in, an analyst notices p = 0.04 during an IPL-match traffic spike and wants to declare victory. That is peeking on an anomalous day; the team holds firm, runs to the pre-set sample, and the effect settles at a non-significant +0.4pp. Peeking would have shipped a phantom win.
lessons.code:
Sample-size rule of thumb (proportion metric, ~80% power, 5% two-sided):
  n_per_variant  ≈  16 · p · (1 - p) / δ²
  (the 16 ≈ (1.96 + 0.84)² × 2, the z-values for 5% significance and 80% power)

Worked example — detect 10% -> 12% (absolute MDE δ = 0.02):
  = 16 * 0.10 * 0.90 / (0.02^2)
  = 16 * 0.09 / 0.0004
  = 1.44 / 0.0004
  ≈ 3,600 users per variant  (≈ 7,200 total for A/B)

Smaller effect costs far more — detect only a 1pp lift (δ = 0.01):
  = 16 * 0.09 / 0.0001  ≈  14,400 per variant  (4x the sample)

Checklist before launch:
  [ ] primary metric declared   [ ] guardrails declared (crash, latency, refunds)
  [ ] sample size + duration fixed IN ADVANCE   [ ] no peeking, no festival overlap
lessons.tryit: You are testing a new checkout button. Baseline purchase conversion is 8%, and the smallest lift worth shipping is 1.5 percentage points (to 9.5%). Using n ≈ 16·p·(1−p)/δ², compute the required sample size per variant, then state one primary metric and two guardrail metrics you would monitor.

mistakes.m: Peeking — repeatedly checking the running test and stopping the instant p-value drops below 0.05.
mistakes.fix: Fix sample size and duration before launch and evaluate only at the end, or use a sequential-testing method built for continuous monitoring.

mistakes.m: Declaring a winner on the primary metric while ignoring damage to other parts of the product.
mistakes.fix: Pre-declare guardrail metrics (crash rate, latency, refunds, revenue) and require the variant to not degrade them before shipping.

mistakes.m: Running or reading the test across a sale or festival spike (Diwali, Big Billion Days), contaminating the baseline.
mistakes.fix: Avoid anomalous periods and run for whole weekly cycles so weekday/weekend and seasonal mix are balanced across groups.

links.t: Multiple comparisons problem (why peeking inflates false positives) — Wikipedia

quiz.q: You repeatedly check a live A/B test and stop it the moment the p-value first dips below 0.05. What is the main consequence?
opts: It correctly increases statistical power
It inflates the false-positive (Type I error) rate, so you 'find' wins that are noise
It has no effect on validity
It reduces the sample size you truly needed
quiz.why: The p-value fluctuates as data arrives; testing at many points and stopping on a favourable dip multiplies your chances of a false positive well beyond the nominal 5%.
quiz.approach: Remember each 'look' is another chance to get unlucky. Fix N and duration in advance, or use a method designed for continuous peeking.

quiz.q: Baseline conversion is 10% and you want to detect a 2pp absolute lift (to 12%) at ~80% power, 5% significance. Using n ≈ 16·p·(1−p)/δ², roughly how many users per variant?
quiz.why: 16 × 0.10 × 0.90 / (0.02²) = 1.44 / 0.0004 ≈ 3,600 per variant. The other options are order-of-magnitude slips from mis-scaling the MDE or forgetting to square δ.
quiz.approach: Plug p and the absolute MDE δ into 16·p(1−p)/δ². Watch the units — δ is an absolute proportion (0.02), not 2.

quiz.q: What is a guardrail metric in a product experiment?
opts: The primary metric you are trying to improve
A metric you must ensure the change does NOT harm, e.g. crash rate or latency
Any vanity metric on the dashboard
The number of users in the control group
quiz.why: Guardrails protect overall product health: even a variant that lifts the primary metric should not ship if it degrades crash rate, latency, refunds or revenue.
quiz.approach: Separate 'the thing I want to move' (primary) from 'the things I must not break' (guardrails); a real win improves the first without harming the second.

lessons.title: From Metrics to Decisions: RICE/ICE, Dashboards & Data Storytelling
lessons.concept:
Analytics only matters when it changes a decision, and the first decision is what to build next. Two lightweight prioritisation frameworks dominate product teams. RICE (created by Sean McBride on Intercom's product team in 2018) scores each idea as Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort — Reach is how many users it touches in a period, Impact is a rough multiplier of how much it moves the metric, Confidence is your certainty as a percentage, and Effort is person-months (it sits in the denominator, so cheaper wins rank higher). ICE (associated with Sean Ellis) is the faster cousin: Impact × Confidence × Ease. Both force you to rank a noisy backlog on a common scale and, crucially, to make your assumptions explicit and debatable rather than deciding by whoever argues loudest in the standup.

Reading a product dashboard well is its own skill. Always ask whether you are looking at a snapshot or a trend, and compare against a baseline or target rather than an absolute number in isolation. Prefer week-on-week and year-on-year over raw day-to-day, because small daily samples are mostly noise. Beware averages that hide their distribution (a mean order value can be dragged by a few whales) and mix-shift / Simpson's paradox, where an overall metric moves only because the blend of segments changed, not because any segment did. When a number jumps, segment before you conclude — by city, by platform, by new-vs-returning — to find where the movement actually lives.

Finally, the analysis has to land with PMs and leadership, which is storytelling, not a data dump. Use the pyramid principle (Barbara Minto's idea): lead with the answer and the recommended action (the "so-what"), then support it. Give every chart one message and a headline that states the takeaway, not just "Weekly orders". Add context — a benchmark, target or prior period — so the audience knows whether a number is good or bad. Close with a clear recommendation and the decision you are asking for. In a busy Indian startup review, the analyst who says "Activation dropped 6 points because the OTP step is failing on 2G — I recommend we ship the fallback login; here's the one chart" beats the one who presents forty slides and no ask.
lessons.example: In a Gurugram marketplace's weekly review, an analyst opens with the recommendation, not the data: "We should prioritise the vernacular onboarding flow next sprint." One chart shows Hindi-preference users activating at 31% versus 52% for English — a clear gap with a benchmark. The RICE table backs it: the vernacular flow scores 4,500 versus 3,200 for a flashier rewards feature, because it reaches far more users at low effort. Leadership approves in five minutes because the story led with the decision and the evidence was one chart, not fifty.
lessons.code:
RICE = (Reach × Impact × Confidence) / Effort
  Reach  = users affected per period (e.g. per quarter)
  Impact = 3 massive, 2 high, 1 medium, 0.5 low, 0.25 minimal
  Conf   = 100% / 80% / 50% (high/medium/low), used as a decimal
  Effort = person-months (denominator -> cheaper ranks higher)

Worked comparison:
  Feature A: 8000 × 2 × 0.8 / 4  = 12800 / 4  = 3,200
  Feature B: 3000 × 3 × 1.0 / 2  =  9000 / 2  = 4,500  <- higher priority

ICE (faster, no effort in denominator):
  ICE = Impact × Confidence × Ease   (each scored, say, 1–10)

Excel: =(Reach*Impact*Confidence)/Effort  then sort descending.
lessons.tryit: Take three features on any backlog you know. For each, estimate Reach, Impact (3/2/1/0.5/0.25), Confidence (100/80/50%) and Effort (person-months), and compute the RICE score in a table. Sort descending, then write a two-sentence recommendation to a PM that leads with which feature to build and why — mentioning one risk in your Confidence estimate.

mistakes.m: Inconsistent units in RICE — mixing per-month and per-quarter Reach, or scoring Effort in days for some items and weeks for others.
mistakes.fix: Fix a single time window and unit for every input across all ideas before scoring, so the ranking is comparable.

mistakes.m: Reacting to day-to-day dashboard noise, or trusting an overall metric that moved only due to mix-shift (Simpson's paradox).
mistakes.fix: Compare against a baseline/target, prefer WoW and YoY over daily wobble, and segment before concluding a real change occurred.

mistakes.m: Presenting a data dump — many charts, no headline, no recommendation — and leaving leadership to draw the conclusion.
mistakes.fix: Use the pyramid principle: lead with the recommendation, give each chart one takeaway headline and a benchmark, and end with a clear ask.

quiz.q: How is the RICE score calculated?
opts: Reach × Impact × Confidence × Effort
(Reach × Impact × Confidence) ÷ Effort
Reach + Impact + Confidence − Effort
Impact × Confidence × Ease
quiz.why: RICE puts Effort in the denominator so that, for equal benefit, cheaper items score higher: (Reach × Impact × Confidence) ÷ Effort. Impact×Confidence×Ease is the ICE formula.
quiz.approach: Group the 'value' factors (Reach, Impact, Confidence) on top and the 'cost' factor (Effort) on the bottom — value per unit of effort.

quiz.q: Feature A: Reach 8000, Impact 2, Confidence 0.8, Effort 4. Feature B: Reach 3000, Impact 3, Confidence 1.0, Effort 2. Which has the higher RICE score?
opts: Feature A (3,200)
Feature B (4,500)
They are equal
Cannot be computed
quiz.why: A = 8000×2×0.8/4 = 3,200; B = 3000×3×1.0/2 = 4,500. B scores higher despite lower reach, because its impact/confidence are higher and effort is lower.
quiz.approach: Compute the numerator (Reach×Impact×Confidence) then divide by Effort for each, and compare — don't judge by Reach alone.

quiz.q: What is the most effective way to present an analysis to leadership?
opts: Dump the full dataset and let them find the insight
Lead with the recommendation/so-what, one takeaway per chart, and a clear ask
Show as many charts as possible to look thorough
Present only verbally, with no numbers at all
quiz.why: The pyramid principle says lead with the answer and the decision you want, support each point with one focused chart plus context, and state a clear recommendation — not an unstructured data dump.
quiz.approach: Start from the decision the audience must make, then work backwards to the minimum evidence that supports it; every slide should earn its place with one message.

lessons.title: Retention, Cohorts & the Stickiness Ratio: Reading Survival Curves Like a PM
lessons.concept:
Retention is the single most honest metric in product analytics because, unlike acquisition, it cannot be bought with a marketing burn. At an Indian marketplace like Meesho or Urban Company, if you acquire 1,00,000 users during a Diwali campaign but only 8,000 come back in week 4, you have not built a product — you have rented attention. A cohort is simply a group of users bucketed by a shared start event (usually signup week or first-order week), and cohort retention analysis tracks each bucket forward in time so that a spike in July acquisitions doesn't contaminate what you see about June's user quality.

There are three distinct definitions of retention and confusing them is the classic analyst error. N-day (or classic) retention counts users active on exactly day N — brutal and noisy, but the standard for social/gaming. Unbounded (or rolling) retention counts users active on day N or any day after — kinder, used when usage is naturally infrequent. Bracket/range retention counts users active at least once within a window (say days 7–13 for 'week 1') — the right choice for e-commerce and food delivery where a user ordering once a week is perfectly healthy. Swiggy would be foolish to judge itself on D1 retention; a Dream11 fantasy-cricket app during IPL, when there is a match to play almost every day, absolutely should.

Stickiness, the DAU/MAU ratio, tells you what fraction of your monthly users show up on an average day — a proxy for habit. Because summing daily-active counts over a month and dividing by MAU yields the average number of active days per monthly user, a stickiness of 0.20 loosely implies the average user engages ~6 days a month. A messaging app like WhatsApp lives at 0.5+ (people open it daily); a used-car platform like CARS24 or a matrimony app is intrinsically low-frequency and might sit at 0.05, which is not a bug but the nature of the category. The cardinal sin is comparing your stickiness to a benchmark from a different frequency category.
lessons.example: A grocery app runs three cohorts. The Jan cohort of 10,000 users retains 4,200 in week 1, 3,100 in week 4, 2,900 in week 8 — the curve flattens, meaning a loyal core has formed. The Feb cohort (acquired via a heavy cashback push) starts at 15,000 but drops to 1,500 by week 4 and keeps falling with no flattening. Same top-line MAU growth, opposite health: the flattening tail is the only signal that matters, and the Feb cohort reveals the cashback bought vanity, not a product.
lessons.tryit: Take any product you use daily and one you use monthly. Estimate your own DAU/MAU for each over the last 30 days (days opened ÷ 30 vs. whether you were monthly-active). Then decide which retention definition — N-day, rolling, or bracket — you'd pick for each, and write one sentence justifying it based on the natural usage frequency.

mistakes.m: Reporting a single 'retention %' without stating the definition (N-day vs bracket vs rolling) or the day/week number.
mistakes.fix: Always label it fully: 'D7 classic retention = 24%' or 'Week-4 bracket retention = 31%'. The number is meaningless without the definition and horizon attached.

mistakes.m: Comparing stickiness (DAU/MAU) across categories — e.g. concluding a car-buying app is 'unhealthy' at 0.05 because a chat app is at 0.5.
mistakes.fix: Only compare stickiness within the same usage-frequency category, or compare a product to its own past. Low-frequency products have structurally low DAU/MAU by design.

mistakes.m: Reading only the first drop of the retention curve and ignoring whether the tail flattens.
mistakes.fix: Judge cohorts by whether the curve flattens into a stable plateau — that plateau is your true retained core. A curve still falling at week 8 has no product-market fit signal yet.

quiz.q: A weekly-groceries app measures success using D1 (next-day) classic retention and panics that it's only 6%. What's the core analytical flaw?
opts: D1 retention is always unreliable and should never be used
The retention definition doesn't match the product's natural usage frequency; bracket/weekly retention fits a once-a-week product
6% is objectively a good number, so there's no problem to solve
They should switch to measuring MAU instead of retention
quiz.why: A once-a-week product will structurally show near-zero next-day retention because users aren't supposed to return daily. The definition must match the intended usage cadence — bracket (weekly) retention is appropriate here. It's not that D1 is universally bad (it's the right lens for a daily-use app), nor that MAU replaces retention.
quiz.approach: Match the retention definition to the product's natural frequency. When a metric looks alarming, first ask whether the measurement window fits how the product is actually meant to be used.

quiz.q: Two cohorts both reach 30% retention at week 4. Cohort A's curve is still steeply declining; Cohort B's has flattened. What's the most defensible read?
opts: They are equally healthy since both are at 30%
Cohort A is healthier because it started higher
Cohort B shows a stabilising retained core; A has not yet demonstrated a durable base
Neither can be judged without knowing acquisition cost
quiz.why: A flattening curve indicates a loyal core that keeps returning — the hallmark of product-market fit. A still-declining curve at the same point may keep falling toward zero, so its week-4 value is a waypoint, not a floor. The shape of the tail, not the single week-4 number, distinguishes them.
quiz.approach: Read retention curves by their asymptote/shape, not a single point. Flattening = durable core; continued decline = no stable base yet.

quiz.q: Stickiness is defined as which ratio, and what does a value of 0.20 mean?
opts: MAU/DAU; the average user is active 20 days a month
DAU/MAU; on an average day about 20% of monthly-active users show up
DAU/WAU; users return every 20th day
New users/Total users; 20% of users are new
quiz.why: Stickiness = DAU/MAU. A value of 0.20 means that on a typical day, roughly one in five of your monthly-active users is present. Because summed daily-actives over MAU equals average active days per monthly user, 0.20 also loosely implies the average user engages ~6 days a month — a signal of habit strength.
quiz.approach: Remember the ratio is DAU over MAU (daily as a share of monthly). Loosely, 0.20 ≈ 6 active days/month for the average user.

lessons.title: The AARRR Funnel & Choosing a North-Star Metric That Can't Be Gamed
lessons.concept:
Dave McClure's AARRR framework — Acquisition, Activation, Retention, Referral, Revenue — nicknamed the 'pirate metrics' funnel, forces you to instrument a product as a sequence of conversions rather than a pile of vanity counts. Acquisition is how users find you (SEO, paid, WhatsApp forwards); Activation is the first moment they experience core value (the 'aha moment'); Retention is whether they come back; Referral is whether they pull others in (critical for Indian D2C growth where referral loops on WhatsApp are cheaper than any ad); Revenue is monetisation. The discipline is measuring the drop-off between each stage, because a funnel where 60% of activated users never retain is a leaky bucket no amount of top-of-funnel spend will fix.

Activation deserves special respect because it is the highest-leverage stage and the one analysts most often define lazily. The 'aha moment' should be an empirically-derived action correlated with long-term retention — famously '7 friends in 10 days' for Facebook, or for an Indian food app it might be 'placed 2nd order within 14 days'. You find it by comparing retained vs. churned users and hunting for the early behaviour that best separates them — not by guessing. Correlation here is a starting hypothesis, not proof of causation, so the honest next step is to try to move that behaviour and see whether retention follows.

A North-Star Metric (NSM) is the single number that best captures the value your product delivers to customers, chosen so that moving it requires genuinely helping users. The test of a good NSM is whether it can be gamed without creating real value. 'Registered users' is a terrible NSM — you can inflate it with a fake-signup incentive. 'Weekly active buyers who received an order' is far better because you cannot fake it without actually delivering value. Spotify uses 'time spent listening'; Airbnb 'nights booked'; a Meesho-style reseller marketplace might use 'weekly orders successfully delivered'. The NSM sits at the value-exchange point of the funnel, and the AARRR stages become its input metrics.
lessons.example: A fintech lending app celebrates crossing 50 lakh app installs (Acquisition) in board slides. But the funnel reveals: 50L installs → 9L complete KYC (Activation) → 1.1L take a loan (Revenue) → 40K repay and take a second loan (Retention). The true product bottleneck is KYC completion, not marketing. Picking 'installs' as the North-Star would keep pouring money into ads; picking 'users who took a 2nd loan' as the NSM redirects the whole company toward fixing KYC drop-off and repayment experience — the stages that actually create durable value.
lessons.tryit: Pick a product you know well. Write out its five AARRR stages with a plausible metric for each. Then propose one North-Star Metric and stress-test it: describe a concrete way a growth-hacker could inflate that number WITHOUT helping any real user. If you can find such a way, the metric is gameable — revise it until you can't.

mistakes.m: Choosing a vanity North-Star like total registered users or cumulative downloads that only ever goes up.
mistakes.fix: Pick a metric anchored at the value-exchange moment (orders delivered, nights booked, minutes listened) that can fall when the product gets worse — a cumulative number that can never decline hides all problems.

mistakes.m: Defining the activation 'aha moment' by intuition rather than data.
mistakes.fix: Derive it empirically: split users into retained vs churned, then find the early action whose presence most strongly separates the two groups. Then validate it can actually be moved — correlation names the candidate, an experiment confirms it.

mistakes.m: Treating AARRR as a one-way waterfall and ignoring that Referral feeds back into Acquisition.
mistakes.fix: Model it as a loop — referrals lower blended CAC by feeding new acquisition. In Indian markets WhatsApp referral loops are often the cheapest, most durable acquisition channel, so instrument them explicitly.

quiz.q: Which of these is the strongest North-Star Metric for a food-delivery marketplace, judged by the 'cannot be gamed without creating value' test?
opts: Total app downloads to date
Number of restaurants listed on the platform
Weekly orders successfully delivered to customers
Total registered user accounts
quiz.why: 'Weekly orders successfully delivered' sits at the value-exchange point — you cannot inflate it without a real customer actually receiving real food, so it resists gaming and it can fall when the product degrades. Downloads, listings, and registrations can all be pumped up with incentives or spam without any user getting value, and they only ever accumulate.
quiz.approach: Apply the gameability test: ask whether the metric can rise without a real user getting real value. The best NSM sits where value is actually exchanged and can fall if the product degrades.

quiz.q: In AARRR, a product has healthy acquisition and revenue-per-user but its retention curve collapses. What does the framework tell you to prioritise?
opts: Spend more on acquisition to outrun the churn
Fix the leaky bucket — retention — before scaling spend, since new users drain out
Increase prices to raise revenue per user
Add more referral incentives to grow the top of funnel
quiz.why: Pouring acquisition into a product that can't retain is filling a leaky bucket — spend scales the leak. AARRR's value is exposing that the binding constraint is retention; fixing it must precede scaling acquisition or referral, both of which just add water to the leak (and referrals from churned users dry up anyway).
quiz.approach: Find the binding constraint in the funnel and fix it before scaling upstream stages. Retention leaks make acquisition spend a losing trade.

quiz.q: What is the correct, rigorous way to define a product's 'activation' (aha) moment?
opts: Whatever action the founder believes represents value
The moment a user completes signup
An early action, found empirically, that best separates users who later retain from those who churn
The first time a user makes any payment
quiz.why: Activation should be derived, not guessed: compare retained vs churned cohorts and identify the early behaviour most predictive of long-term retention (e.g. '2nd order in 14 days'). Signup and first payment are sometimes it, but only if the data shows they predict retention — the empirical method, not any specific action, is the point.
quiz.approach: Define activation empirically by contrasting retained and churned users. Let the data name the predictive early action rather than asserting it.

lessons.title: A/B Testing for Products: Sample Size, Peeking, Guardrails & Novelty
lessons.concept:
A product A/B test (online controlled experiment) randomly splits users into control and treatment, ships a change to treatment, and asks whether the difference in a chosen metric is real or noise. The statistical machinery matters because human intuition is catastrophically bad at distinguishing signal from randomness — a 3% lift on 500 users is almost always noise. Three numbers govern any test: the baseline conversion rate, the minimum detectable effect (MDE — the smallest lift worth caring about), and statistical power (conventionally 80%), together with the significance level α (conventionally 5%). Crucially, the smaller the effect you want to detect, the larger the sample you need — because required sample scales with 1/MDE², halving your MDE roughly quadruples the required sample size, which is why testing tiny changes on a low-traffic Indian B2B product can be practically impossible.

The most damaging and widespread malpractice is 'peeking' — repeatedly checking the p-value and stopping the moment it dips below 0.05. Every peek is another roll of the dice at a false positive, so continuous peeking with a fixed-horizon test can inflate your real false-positive rate from 5% to well over 25%. The disciplines that prevent this: fix your sample size and test duration in advance and don't stop early, or use methods explicitly designed for continuous monitoring (sequential testing / always-valid p-values, or a Bayesian framework). Also run at least one full weekly cycle — Indian usage on Monday differs sharply from Sunday, and festival weeks distort everything.

Guardrail metrics are the safety rails: metrics you don't intend to improve but refuse to harm. If you're testing a checkout change to lift conversion, page-load latency, refund rate, and app-crash rate are guardrails — a 'winning' variant that lifts conversion 2% while doubling crashes is a loss. Finally, beware the novelty effect (users click the shiny new button just because it's new; the lift decays) and its twin, change aversion (loyal users resist any change at first, so an early dip may recover) — both mean the first few days of a test can badly mislead, another reason to run the full pre-planned duration.
lessons.example: A marketplace tests a new 'Buy Now' button colour. On day 2 the analyst sees +8% conversion, p=0.04, and messages the PM to ship it. This is textbook peeking on an underpowered early sample. The team instead holds to the pre-computed 14-day, 60,000-user-per-arm plan. By day 14 the lift has settled to +0.6% (p=0.21 — not significant), and the early spike is revealed as a novelty effect plus random noise. Shipping on day 2 would have been a phantom win, and worse, would have taught the org to trust peeking.
lessons.tryit: Using the rule-of-thumb n ≈ 16·p(1−p)/MDE², compute the per-arm sample size for a product with a 10% baseline conversion where you want to detect a +1 percentage-point absolute lift (to 11%). Then recompute for a +0.5pp lift and confirm for yourself that the sample roughly quadruples. Note how many days of your product's traffic each would take.

mistakes.m: Peeking at results daily and stopping the test the first time p dips below 0.05.
mistakes.fix: Fix sample size and duration up front and don't stop early — or adopt sequential/always-valid or Bayesian methods built for continuous monitoring. Repeated peeking on a fixed-horizon test massively inflates the false-positive rate.

mistakes.m: Optimising only the primary metric and ignoring what the change breaks elsewhere.
mistakes.fix: Define guardrail metrics (latency, crash rate, refunds, unsubscribes) before launch and refuse to ship a variant that wins the primary metric but harms a guardrail.

mistakes.m: Ending the test after 2-3 days and trusting an early spike.
mistakes.fix: Run at least one full weekly cycle (ideally the pre-computed duration) to average out day-of-week effects and let novelty / change-aversion effects decay before judging the result.

links.t: Statistical power (Power of a test) — Wikipedia

quiz.q: An analyst checks an A/B test's p-value every day and ships the moment it first drops below 0.05 on day 3. Why is this conclusion untrustworthy?
opts: P-values can never be used to make ship decisions
Repeated peeking with a fixed-horizon test inflates the real false-positive rate well above the nominal 5%
Day 3 is always too early regardless of sample size reached
The test needs at least 1 million users to be valid
quiz.why: Each peek is another opportunity to cross the significance threshold by chance, so continuously peeking and stopping at the first p<0.05 can drive the true Type-I error to 20-30%+ over a two-week daily-look test — far above the nominal 5%. The fix is a pre-fixed horizon or a sequential/always-valid/Bayesian method designed for continuous looks — not that p-values are unusable or that a fixed giant sample is required.
quiz.approach: Recognise peeking as a multiple-comparisons problem: many looks multiply chances of a false positive. Pre-commit to sample size/duration, or use always-valid methods.

quiz.q: A checkout redesign lifts conversion by a significant +1.5%, but app crash rate rises from 0.4% to 0.9% in the treatment arm. What's the right call?
opts: Ship it — conversion is the primary metric and it won
Ship it but monitor crashes afterwards
Do not ship as-is — the crash rate is a guardrail metric that was breached
Re-run the test with a larger sample to confirm the conversion lift
quiz.why: Crash rate is a guardrail — a metric you refuse to harm even while chasing a primary win. More than doubling crashes (0.4%→0.9%) negates the value of a conversion lift and damages long-term trust and retention. A variant that wins the primary metric but breaches a guardrail should not ship as-is; fix the crash regression first, then re-test.
quiz.approach: Evaluate experiments on primary AND guardrail metrics jointly. A guardrail breach blocks the ship regardless of the primary result.

quiz.q: To detect a smaller effect (halving your minimum detectable effect) at the same power and significance, roughly what happens to the required sample size?
opts: It halves
It stays the same
It roughly doubles
It roughly quadruples
quiz.why: Required sample size scales with 1/MDE², so halving the MDE multiplies the sample by about four. This is why detecting tiny lifts demands enormous traffic and is often infeasible for low-traffic products — the analyst must decide up front whether the smallest worthwhile effect is even detectable.
quiz.approach: Remember sample size ∝ 1/MDE². Smaller effects cost quadratically more traffic; set your MDE to the smallest lift that's actually worth shipping.

lessons.title: From Event Instrumentation to Boardroom: RICE Prioritisation & the Data Story
lessons.concept:
Everything downstream — dashboards, funnels, experiments — is only as trustworthy as the event instrumentation beneath it. An event is a user action captured with a name and properties (e.g. order_placed with properties {value: 499, payment_mode: 'UPI', city: 'Pune'}). The discipline that separates a professional analytics stack from chaos is a tracking plan: a governed schema defining every event name, its properties, and their types, agreed before engineers write the logging code. Without it you get the classic Indian-startup mess — 'Order Placed', 'order_placed', 'placeOrder', and 'OrderComplete' all firing from different screens, making any funnel un-analysable. Pick one convention (snake_case, past-tense object-action) and enforce it; track events not just pageviews; and never bury a value you'll want to segment on inside the event name — put city as a property, not as separate events order_placed_pune and order_placed_mumbai.

Once you can trust the data, you must prioritise what to build, and RICE gives a defensible score: Reach × Impact × Confidence ÷ Effort. Reach is how many users are affected per time period (e.g. per quarter); Impact is a per-user magnitude on a fixed scale (3 = massive, 2 = high, 1 = medium, 0.5 = low, 0.25 = minimal); Confidence is a percentage haircut (100% / 80% / 50%) reflecting how much evidence backs your Reach and Impact estimates; Effort is person-months. Dividing by Effort makes it a return-on-effort ranking, and the Confidence term is what stops a charismatic PM's gut-feel pet project from outscoring an evidence-backed one. ICE (Impact, Confidence, Ease) is the lighter cousin for faster, rougher calls.

Finally, the highest-paid skill in analytics is telling the data story to leadership, because a correct analysis nobody acts on has zero value. Lead with the 'so what', not the methodology — executives want the decision and its implication first, the supporting chart second, the caveats third (the pyramid principle: answer first). Always pair a metric with its comparison (vs last month, vs target, vs cohort) because a number without context is unreadable, and always translate to money or users, never leave it at 'p=0.03'. Show absolute numbers alongside percentages — '+40%' on a base of 50 users is noise, and a sharp CFO will ask.
lessons.example: A PM must choose between three features. Feature A (one-tap reorder): Reach 40,000 users/quarter, Impact 2 (high), Confidence 80%, Effort 2 person-months → RICE = (40000×2×0.8)/2 = 32,000. Feature B (dark mode): Reach 60,000, Impact 0.5, Confidence 100%, Effort 3 → (60000×0.5×1.0)/3 = 10,000. Feature C (loyalty programme): Reach 30,000, Impact 3, Confidence 50%, Effort 6 → (30000×3×0.5)/6 = 7,500. Reorder wins decisively — and when the loyalty-programme champion protests, the Confidence 50% term makes the reason explicit: the impact is a guess, not evidence.
lessons.tryit: Score two features you'd want in an app you use, using RICE. Assign Reach (users/quarter), Impact (3/2/1/0.5/0.25), Confidence (100/80/50%) and Effort (person-months) for each, compute both scores, and rank them. Then write the one-sentence 'so what' you'd open a leadership meeting with — decision first, backed by a single number that includes its comparison point.

mistakes.m: Encoding segmentable values into event names (order_placed_upi, order_placed_cod) instead of using properties.
mistakes.fix: Keep one event (order_placed) and put the variable dimension as a property ({payment_mode: 'upi'}). This lets you segment freely later; baking values into names makes the taxonomy explode and funnels un-buildable.

mistakes.m: In RICE, dropping the Confidence term so a well-argued but unevidenced pet feature scores as high as a data-backed one.
mistakes.fix: Always apply the Confidence haircut (100/80/50%). It is precisely the mechanism that discounts guesses relative to evidence and keeps prioritisation honest against persuasive advocacy.

mistakes.m: Presenting to leadership methodology-first and burying the recommendation, or quoting percentages with no base and no comparison.
mistakes.fix: Lead with the decision and its 'so what' (answer-first / pyramid principle), pair every metric with a comparison (vs target/last period), show absolute numbers next to percentages, and translate to money or users.

links.t: Barbara Minto & the Pyramid Principle — Wikipedia

quiz.q: Two features: X has Reach 50,000, Impact 1, Confidence 100%, Effort 2. Y has Reach 20,000, Impact 3, Confidence 50%, Effort 3. Which wins on RICE and by how much?
opts: They tie at 25,000 each
quiz.why: X = (50000×1×1.0)/2 = 25,000. Y = (20000×3×0.5)/3 = 10,000. X wins clearly. The Confidence 50% haircut on Y (its impact is a guess) is decisive — without it Y would score 20,000 and look far closer, illustrating exactly why Confidence belongs in the formula.
quiz.approach: Compute RICE = (R×I×C)/E for each and compare. Watch how the Confidence term penalises low-evidence estimates — that's its whole purpose.

quiz.q: An engineer proposes logging order_placed_pune, order_placed_mumbai, and order_placed_delhi as three separate events. Why is this poor instrumentation?
opts: Event names must always be in uppercase
City is a segmentable dimension and belongs as a property on a single order_placed event, not baked into event names
You should never track the city a user is in
Three events are always fewer than one event with properties
quiz.why: City is a dimension you'll want to slice by — it belongs as a property {city: 'pune'} on one order_placed event. Encoding it into the event name makes the taxonomy explode (every new city adds events), breaks a single clean funnel, and makes 'total orders' require summing many events. The fix is one event, many properties.
quiz.approach: Put variable dimensions in event properties, not event names. One well-propertied event segments infinitely; name-encoded values fragment your taxonomy.

quiz.q: Presenting a retention analysis to the CEO, which opening is strongest per answer-first (pyramid) storytelling?
opts: 'Let me walk you through our cohort methodology and SQL joins first.'
'Week-4 retention rose from 18% to 26% after onboarding changes — we should roll it out to all users; that's ~12,000 more retained users a month.'
'Retention went up by a lot this quarter, it's really exciting.'
'Here are twelve charts covering every segment we analysed.'
quiz.why: The strong opening leads with the decision ('roll it out'), pairs the metric with its comparison (18%→26%), and translates to users (~12,000/month). Methodology-first buries the point, vague enthusiasm gives no number, and a wall of charts has no 'so what'. Answer-first respects the executive's time and drives a decision.
quiz.approach: Open with the recommendation and its quantified impact (with a comparison point); relegate methodology and caveats to later. Decision first, evidence second, caveats last.
